description: Create a two-column content slide on the US near-term economic outlook with a scenario comparison table on the left and a bullet list of what to watch for on the right.

header.headline: The base case for US economy is continued bifurcation that leads to slowing and relatively jobless growth, with risk that tariff-led inflation and AI market correction trigger stagflation
header.sub: US near-term economic outlook and key scenarios

rows: Broad-based slowing of consumer spending and pullback in AI capex; Delayed impacts from tariffs and immigration restrictions keep inflation above 3%; Fed lowers rates below “neutral” if growth decline occurs alongside financial stress

Bifurcation of economy continues, with growth decelerating to 1.5-2% amidst continued solid AI capex but softening consumer spending; Investment-led growth limits job gains; Fed eases rates further to cushion growth but stops at ~3% neutral rate

Growth remains above trend on the back of AI productivity boost and broader recovery in non-AI investment due to falling interest rates, tax cuts and reshoring; Stronger demand leads Fed to keep rates above neutral to manage inflation risks

Larger and more persistent increase in inflation from tariffs; Geopolitical conflict(s) trigger renewed supply chain disruptions; Credit stress episode triggered by correction in AI company valuations
Tariff boost to inflation is short-lived; Ongoing affordability pressures and depleted household savings dampen consumption; No major drop-off in AI capex or valuations
OBBBA tax cuts and deregulation spur rebound in business investment beyond AI; Larger and faster-than-expected boost to productivity from AI; Stabilizing labor market, low AI displacement
Rising bankruptcies denting B2B demand and household finances
Further margin erosion from softer demand and weaker pricing power
Stabilizing margins; Tighter labor and financing conditions

items.sub: Ongoing affordability pressures and labor market weakening could further widen the divide in purchasing power and sentiment between lower- and higher-income consumers
Massive AI-related investments drove a large share of GDP growth in 2025, but these may begin to slow in 2026 due to power constraints or delayed catch-up in AI demand
Companies’ pre-tariff inventory buffers dwindle; the inflation and supply chain impacts of the large tariff increases to date (with more in pipeline for 2026) could intensify
Potential for increased layoffs as consumer demand weakens, as well as transitional unemployment due to AI adoption, weakening employment rates for new college graduates, and immigration restrictions
Loss of confidence around pace of AI adoption and AI company earnings could trigger sharp decline in valuations, with knock-on wealth effects that weigh on higher-income consumer spend
